- commit-commands:clean_gone: Cleans up all git branches marked as [gone] (branches that have been deleted on the remote but still exist locally), including removing associated worktrees.
- commit-commands:commit-push-pr: Commit, push, and open a PR
- commit-commands:commit: Create a git commit
- claude-code-setup:claude-automation-recommender: Analyze a codebase and recommend Claude Code automations (hooks, subagents, skills, plugins, MCP servers). Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## Search Results for Betterleaks Secret Scanning Tool

Here's what I found about Betterleaks:

### Overview

Betterleaks is a new open-source tool that can scan directories, files, and git repositories and identify valid secrets using default or customized rules. The project is intended as a more advanced successor to Gitleaks and is maintained by the same team, with support from Aikido, a Belgian company that provides a platform for securing the development cycle.

### Key Features

Betterleaks introduces a technique called Token Efficiency based on byte pair encoding (BPE) tokenization, which measures how a BPE tokenizer compresses a given string. Natural language compresses well into longer tokens, producing high token efficiency, while secrets and random strings compress poorly, producing low token efficiency. Betterleaks uses this to filter out false positives. Against the CredData dataset, Token Efficiency achieved 98.6 percent recall compared to 70.4 percent for entropy.

Validation logic in Betterleaks is written using the Common Expression Language (CEL), giving rule authors programmatic control over what counts as a confirmed secret. The tool also handles doubly and triply encoded secrets by default and supports parallelized git scanning to reduce scan times.

### Drop-in Replacement for Gitleaks

Betterleaks is built to function as a drop-in replacement for Gitleaks, meaning existing CLI flags and configuration files carry over without modification.

### Future Roadmap

The developer has revealed additional features planned for the next version of Betterleaks, like support for additional data sources beyond Git repositories and files, LLM-assisted analysis for better secret classification, more detection filters, automatic secret revocation via provider APIs, permissions mapping, and performance optimizations.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

### Overview

Betterleaks is a new open-source tool that can scan directories, files, and git repositories and identify valid secrets using default or customized rules. The project is intended as a more advanced successor to Gitleaks and is maintained by the same team, with support from Aikido, a Belgian company that provides a platform for securing the development cycle.

content.text:
Here's where things stand on both fronts:

**Push Protection: ❌ disabled.** In fact, GitHub Secret Scanning itself is disabled too. The repo is **public**, so both are free to enable — you can flip them on in Settings → Code security, or I can do it via API (`gh api -X PATCH`). Worth doing regardless of the gitleaks/betterleaks question, since it's a server-side net that catches pushes the client-side hook can't.

Now let me verify the exact betterleaks pre-commit hook + Action coordinates before I edit anything (I don't want to guess repo paths/versions).

content.text: Got what I need. Key finding: betterleaks has a pre-commit hook (id `betterleaks`, latest `v1.4.1`) but **no official GitHub Action** (no `betterleaks-action` repo, no `action.yml`) — so the CI job can't be a one-line `uses:` swap like gitleaks was. Let me check the recommended CI approach.

input.prompt: How do you run betterleaks in CI / GitHub Actions? Is there an official GitHub Action, or do you install the binary or run the Docker image? What is the exact recommended command to scan a git repo in CI, and what exit code does it return when secrets are found?

content.content:
# Betterleaks CI/CD Integration

Based on the provided documentation, the page does not address CI/CD integration, GitHub Actions setup, or exit code behavior for secret detection.

The only CI-related information mentioned is a usage example showing how to exclude automated bot contributions: `attributes[?"git.author_name"].orValue("") == "renovate[bot]"` — suggesting awareness of CI contexts, but no guidance on implementation.

For repository scanning, the documentation shows: `"betterleaks git /path/to/repo -v --git-workers=16"` as a general command structure, but contains no details about CI/CD deployment methods, official GitHub Actions, or what exit codes indicate findings.

**Recommendation:** Consult the project's GitHub repository, scanning documentation, or contact the maintainers for CI/CD-specific guidance, as this information isn't covered in the provided web page content.

So the CI job becomes a container run instead of a `uses:` step. Let me make the changes. First the two functional files:

secrets:
    name: Secret scanning (betterleaks)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      # Betterleaks has no official GitHub Action; run the pinned container image.
      # Drop-in gitleaks replacement: nonzero exit on findings fails the job.
      - name: Run betterleaks
        run: |
          docker run --rm -v "$PWD:/repo" -w /repo \
            ghcr.io/betterleaks/betterleaks:v1.4.1 \
            git /repo --redact --verbose

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

concurrency:
  group: ci-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  # ── Backend ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  backend-lint:
    name: Backend — lint & format
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install lint tools
        run: pip install -r backend/requirements-tooling.txt

      - name: Black (format check)
        run: black --check backend/

      - name: Ruff (lint)
        run: ruff check backend/

      - name: Typecheck-deps in sync
        # Guard the pre-commit mypy hook against backend/requirements-typecheck.txt
        # (Dependabot bumps the requirements file but bypasses the local hook —
        # this catches the drift in CI, which previously slipped through).
        run: python scripts/check-typecheck-deps.py

  backend-types:
    name: Backend — type check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install type-check deps
        # mypy from the tooling file; the typed subset it checks against.
        run: |
          pip install -r backend/requirements-tooling.txt
          pip install -r backend/requirements-typecheck.txt

      - name: mypy
        run: mypy backend/app/

  backend-sast:
    name: Backend — SAST (Bandit)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install Bandit
        run: pip install -r backend/requirements-tooling.txt

      - name: Bandit
        run: bandit -c pyproject.toml -r backend/app/

  backend-audit:
    name: Backend — dependency audit (pip-audit)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install pip-audit
        run: pip install pip-audit==2.10.0

      - name: pip-audit (full runtime + test surface)
        # Synchronous CVE gate over the entire runtime + test dependency tree
        # (single source: requirements-dev.txt) + transitive deps, against the
        # PyPI/OSV advisory DB. Complements the Dependabot alerts (full graph,
        # async) with a fail-fast merge check.
        #
        # The 5 ignored advisories are cryptography + pyOpenSSL CVEs blocked by
        # snowflake-connector-python<4 / snowflake-sqlalchemy<4 — a deliberate
        # major datasource bump tracked in #129 (the deadline for removing these).
        run: |
          pip-audit -r backend/requirements-dev.txt \
            --ignore-vuln PYSEC-2026-36 \
            --ignore-vuln PYSEC-2026-35 \
            --ignore-vuln CVE-2026-26007 \
            --ignore-vuln CVE-2026-27448 \
            --ignore-vuln CVE-2026-27459

  backend-test:
    name: Backend — tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Ephemeral Postgres for DB-backed integration tests (seed/run/endpoint).
    # Matches the compose image; gen_random_uuid() is built in to PG16.
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_USER: dataq
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: dataq
          POSTGRES_DB: dataq_test
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U dataq"
          --health-interval 5s
          --health-timeout 3s
          --health-retries 10
      # Redis broker for the real-broker E2E (dispatch -> worker round-trip).
      redis:
        image: redis:7-alpine
        ports:
          - 6379:6379
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "redis-cli ping"
          --health-interval 5s
          --health-timeout 3s
          --health-retries 10
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install test deps
        # Single source of truth: runtime + typed subset + test toolchain, all
        # from backend/requirements-dev.txt. pip upgraded for PYSEC-2026-196.
        run: |
          python -m pip install -U pip
          pip install -r backend/requirements-dev.txt

      - name: pytest
        # TEST_DATABASE_URL enables the db_session fixture; DATABASE_URL + REDIS_URL
        # enable the real-broker E2E. All DB/broker tests skip when unset (e.g. a
        # contributor running pytest without the services).
        env:
          TEST_DATABASE_URL: REDACTED
          DATABASE_URL: REDACTED
          REDIS_URL: redis://localhost:6379/0
        run: pytest backend/tests/

  # ── Secret scanning ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  secrets:
    name: Secret scanning (gitleaks)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v3
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

  # ── Frontend ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  frontend-lint:
    name: Frontend — lint & format
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v6
        with:
          version: 9

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: Prettier (format check)
        run: pnpm format:check

      - name: ESLint
        run: pnpm lint

      - name: TypeScript
        run: pnpm typecheck

  frontend-test:
    name: Frontend — tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v6
        with:
          version: 9

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: Vitest
        run: pnpm test

  frontend-audit:
    name: Frontend — dependency audit (pnpm audit)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v6
        with:
          version: 9

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: pnpm audit (high+)
        # Fails on high/critical advisories in the dependency tree; lower
        # severities are reported but don't block (avoid noise from unfixable
        # transitive dev-tool advisories). Pairs with Dependabot alerts (async).
        run: pnpm audit --audit-level=high

  # ── CodeQL SAST ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  codeql:
    name: CodeQL analysis
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      security-events: write
      actions: read
      contents: read
    strategy:
      matrix:
        language: [python, javascript-typescript]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: github/codeql-action/init@v4
        with:
          languages: ${{ matrix.language }}
          config-file: ./.github/codeql/codeql-config.yml

      - uses: github/codeql-action/autobuild@v4

      - uses: github/codeql-action/analyze@v4

repos:
  # ── Linting + import sorting (runs first so Black formats the fixed code) ──
  - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit
    rev: v0.15.14
    hooks:
      - id: ruff
        args: [--fix]

  # ── Formatting (canonical formatter — runs last to own final layout) ───────
  - repo: https://github.com/psf/black
    rev: 26.5.1
    hooks:
      - id: black
        language_version: python3.11

  # ── Type checking ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/mirrors-mypy
    rev: v2.1.0
    hooks:
      - id: mypy
        files: ^backend/app/
        # Must match backend/requirements-typecheck.txt exactly.
        # Drift is caught by the typecheck-deps-sync hook below.
        additional_dependencies:

The re-listed subsets `requirements-dev.txt` pulls are `requirements-typecheck.txt` (the typed deps mypy needs) and `requirements-tooling.txt` (Black/Ruff/mypy/Bandit/pre-commit); the `typecheck-deps-sync` check (pre-commit **and** CI `backend-lint`) keeps the mypy hook aligned. `requirements-mutation.txt` (mutmut) is **standalone — not `-r`'d by anything**, so it stays off CI's install + `pip-audit` surface (manual tool, CONTRIBUTING rule 4a). Bump a Python version in `requirements.txt` only.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# ADR 0013 — Marketplace distribution (customer-deployed BYOL) and anti-vendor-lock-in guardrails

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-01
- **Deciders:** @TheurgicDuke771

## Context

We are evaluating listing DataQ on the **Azure, AWS, and GCP marketplaces**. Two questions follow: is it viable given the current architecture, and what must change to keep the option open without derailing the 8-week v1.

There are two fundamentally different ways to be on a cloud marketplace, with very different engineering cost:

| | **Vendor-hosted SaaS** | **Customer-deployed (BYOL)** |
|---|---|---|
| We operate | one multi-tenant service | nothing — the customer deploys into *their own* subscription/account |
| Tenancy | **multi-tenant required** | single-tenant is fine — one isolated instance per customer |
| Billing | marketplace metering-API integration | "bring your own license", billed outside the meter |
| Auth | customer IdP federates into ours | customer's own IdP, in their tenant |
| Fit for DataQ | ❌ requires a tenant-isolation rebuild | ✅ aligns with the current design |

DataQ v1 is **explicitly single-tenant** (CLAUDE.md §1). Vendor-hosted multi-tenant SaaS would force tenant isolation into every table, per-tenant secrets, and tenant-scoped auth — a large rebuild that fights the current architecture. Customer-deployed BYOL is the opposite: single-tenant *is* the unit of sale, one isolated deployment per customer.

The architecture is already partly portable by intent — [ADR 0010](0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md) established that **Azure is the default implementation of each infra seam, not the architecture** (secrets behind a Protocol, auth behind a generic `current_user` boundary, observability containable to one handler, hosting already container-portable). [ADR 0011](0011-extensibility-seams-for-deferred-integrations.md) did the same on the feature side. This ADR records the *distribution decision* those seams serve, and closes the remaining lock-in gaps the marketplace analysis surfaced (packaging, datasource credential auth, an explicit standing checklist).

## Decision

**1. Distribution model = customer-deployed BYOL, not vendor-hosted multi-tenant SaaS.** Each customer deploys an isolated DataQ instance into their own cloud account; we do not operate a shared multi-tenant service. This preserves the single-tenant design rather than rebuilding it.

**2. Marketplace listing is a post-v1 initiative. No marketplace work enters the 8-week v1 budget.** v1 ships Azure-hosted and single-tenant exactly as planned. The only obligation v1 carries is the *negative* one in guardrail 3: do not deepen Azure coupling.

**3. Standing anti-lock-in guardrails — binding on v1 PRs now, even though marketplace work is deferred.** Keep Azure as *one implementation behind each seam*; never let a vendor-specific assumption leak into business logic. These are the rule future PRs are reviewed against:

| Seam | Guardrail | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Auth** | Protected routes depend on the generic `get_current_user` dependency (mode-bound in `core/auth.py`); **never read MSAL/Entra claims in route or service code**. Azure AD is one OIDC provider, not *the* auth. (extends [ADR 0010](0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md) §2) | discipline now; generic OIDC `AuthProvider` impl deferred |
| **Secrets** | New backends (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Vault) are additive `SecretStore` classes; no call site changes. (ADR 0010 §1) | already abstracted |
| **Observability** | App Insights stays behind the `core/logging.py` handler; second backend arrives via **OpenTelemetry/OTLP**, not a parallel hardcoded path. (ADR 0010 §3) | seam reserved |
| **Packaging** | Container images stay cloud-neutral — no Azure-only assumptions baked into app code or entrypoints. The portable deploy artifact is a **Helm chart** (build deferred). | images portable; chart deferred |
| **Datasource auth** | Credential modes stay behind `ConnectionAdapter`; Workload Identity / IAM-role / Managed-Identity are additive per cloud (already the Week-7 plan, SAS/access-key only in v1). | seam exists |
| **Config** | No hardcoded Azure resource names/endpoints in business logic — everything via Pydantic Settings (12-factor). | in place |

**4. Phasing (post-v1).** Sequenced so the portability spend happens once and unlocks both non-Azure clouds together:
   - **Phase 1 — Azure Marketplace, BYOL.** Smallest delta; we already run on Azure. Container/Managed-Application offer via Partner Center.
   - **Phase 2 — portability investment:** Helm chart + published images, generic OIDC auth, OTel observability, AWS/GCP `SecretStore` impls, managed-dependency mapping (RDS/Cloud SQL/Azure DB; ElastiCache/Memorystore/Azure Cache).
   - **Phase 3 — AWS, then GCP.** Mostly packaging + per-marketplace onboarding once Phase 2 lands.

**5. Commercial/legal/operational scope is part of the post-v1 initiative, not architecture** — recorded here so it is not forgotten: licensing model + entitlement/license-key (we are not metering under BYOL), seller registration + tax/banking, EULA / privacy / DPA, defined support SLA, per-marketplace security review, and (for enterprise buyers) SOC 2 + pen test. The existing CI scanning (Bandit, CodeQL, gitleaks, Dependabot) is a head start on the security reviews, not a substitute.

## Consequences

**Positive**
- Single-tenant — today's "limitation" — becomes the *product unit* under BYOL; no multi-tenancy rebuild.
- v1 scope and timeline are untouched; the only v1 cost is review discipline that ADR 0010 already imposes.
- When the initiative starts, the portability work is a localized fill-in of documented seams, not a re-architecture.
- One Helm chart + OIDC + OTel covers AKS/EKS/GKE, collapsing three per-cloud engineering stories into one investment.

**Negative**
- DataQ is **not** marketplace-ready at v1 ship — Phase 2's missing implementations (OIDC `AuthProvider`, OTLP exporter, multi-cloud `SecretStore`, Helm chart) are real work. Accepted: marketplace is explicitly post-v1.
- The auth and packaging guardrails rely on developer/review discipline until the formal seams exist. Mitigated by this ADR + ADR 0010 + code review.
- BYOL forgoes marketplace-metered usage billing; revenue is licensed outside the meter. Accepted as the cost of avoiding a multi-tenant rebuild.

## Alternatives considered

- **Vendor-hosted multi-tenant SaaS** — rejected for v1-era scope. Requires retrofitting tenant isolation into the schema, secrets, and auth — a major rebuild against the single-tenant design, for a revenue model (metered SaaS) we can approximate with BYOL licensing.
- **List on all three clouds at once** — rejected. The Azure coupling is deepest; AWS/GCP need the Phase-2 portability work first. Parallelizing triples onboarding/security-review effort with no shared learning.
- **Do nothing / decide at marketplace time** — rejected. Cheap to keep the door open *now* (guardrail 3 is mostly already ADR-0010 discipline); expensive to undo Azure coupling that spread through dozens of endpoints in the interim. The whole point is to pay near-zero during v1 to keep the option live.

## Related

- [ADR 0010](0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md) — provider-agnostic infra seams (the per-seam discipline this distribution decision depends on).
- [ADR 0011](0011-extensibility-seams-for-deferred-integrations.md) — feature-side extensibility seams (connectors, `ResultPublisher`, dbt-as-provider).
- CLAUDE.md §1 (single-tenant scope), §9 (decision table), §11 (anti-patterns — "don't bypass the abstraction").

- **Phase 3 — AWS, then GCP.** Mostly packaging + per-marketplace onboarding once Phase 2 lands.

-**5. Commercial/legal/operational scope is part of the post-v1 initiative, not architecture** — recorded here so it is not forgotten: licensing model + entitlement/license-key (we are not metering under BYOL), seller registration + tax/banking, EULA / privacy / DPA, defined support SLA, per-marketplace security review, and (for enterprise buyers) SOC 2 + pen test. The existing CI scanning (Bandit, CodeQL, gitleaks, Dependabot) is a head start on the security reviews, not a substitute.

The only remaining "gitleaks" mention is an intentional explanatory comment in CI. Everything's swapped and both YAML files parse.

## Done — summary

**1. Switched secret scanning gitleaks → betterleaks** (verified legitimate first: it's from the gitleaks creator, backed by Aikido, an explicit drop-in replacement — not a typosquat). Latest release `v1.4.1` (2026-06-06). Changes:

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| [.pre-commit-config.yaml](.pre-commit-config.yaml#L45) | hook → `betterleaks/betterleaks` `rev: v1.4.1`, id `betterleaks` |
| [.github/workflows/ci.yml](.github/workflows/ci.yml#L164) | CI job now runs the pinned container `ghcr.io/betterleaks/betterleaks:v1.4.1` |
| [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md#L59), [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md#L147), [CLAUDE.md:306](CLAUDE.md#L306), [ADR 0013](docs/adr/0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md#L47) | prose references updated |

**One thing to flag:** betterleaks has **no official GitHub Action** (unlike `gitleaks-action@v3`), so I couldn't do a one-line `uses:` swap in CI. I pinned the official Docker image and run `git /repo --redact --verbose` — as a gitleaks drop-in it exits nonzero on findings, which fails the job. If you'd rather install the binary directly instead of pulling a container, say so.

I haven't run `pre-commit autoupdate`/install (it builds the Go hook) or committed anything — let me know if you want either.

**2. GitHub Secret Scanning Push Protection: ❌ disabled.**

In fact native **secret scanning itself is disabled** too. The repo is **public**, so both are free. They're a server-side net (catches what bypasses the client-side hook — e.g. `--no-verify`, or pushes from machines without pre-commit installed), so worth enabling alongside betterleaks rather than instead of it. I can turn both on via:

```
gh api -X PATCH repos/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ \
  -f 'security_and_analysis[secret_scanning][status]=enabled' \
  -f 'security_and_analysis[secret_scanning_push_protection][status]=enabled'
```

Want me to run that?

Sources: [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/betterleaks-a-new-open-source-secrets-scanner-to-replace-gitleaks/) · [The New Stack](https://thenewstack.io/betterleaks-open-source-secret-scanner/) · [github.com/betterleaks/betterleaks](https://github.com/betterleaks/betterleaks)

content.content: Launching skill: code-review

content.text:
`medium effort → 3+4 angles × 6 candidates → 1-vote verify → ≤8 findings`

You are reviewing for **precision** at medium effort: every finding you surface
should be one a maintainer would act on.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle, up to 6 each)

Run **7 independent finder angles** via the Agent tool. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Name the cheaper alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

Cleanup and altitude candidates use the same `file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in
`failure_scenario`, state the concrete cost (what is duplicated, wasted, or
harder to maintain) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank
cleanup and altitude findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates bypass the verify step and are the
dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Verify (1-vote, 3-state)

Dedup candidates that point at the same line/mechanism, keeping the one with
the most concrete failure scenario. For each remaining candidate, run **one
verifier** via the Agent tool: give it the diff, the relevant
file(s), and the candidate, and have it return exactly one of:

- **CONFIRMED** — can name the inputs/state that trigger it and the wrong
  output or crash. Quote the line.
- **PLAUSIBLE** — mechanism is real, trigger is uncertain (timing, env,
  config). State what would confirm it.
- **REFUTED** — factually wrong (code doesn't say that) or guarded elsewhere.
  Quote the line that proves it.

Keep candidates where the vote is CONFIRMED or PLAUSIBLE.

## Output

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 8 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 8 survive, keep the 8 most
severe. If nothing survives verification, return `[]`.

content.text:
Review complete. The diff is mostly a clean mechanical swap (docs + config). One real risk in the CI job survived verification:

```json
[
  {
    "file": ".github/workflows/ci.yml",
    "line": 178,
    "summary": "Dockerized git scan may fail with 'detected dubious ownership' because the container runs as root (uid 0) while actions/checkout writes files as the runner user (uid 1001).",
    "failure_scenario": "If betterleaks shells out to git (rather than using a pure-Go git impl), git's safe.directory check fires on the uid mismatch between /repo's owner and root → 'fatal: detected dubious ownership in repository at /repo', the scan aborts, and the job either errors or — worse — exits 0 without scanning anything, silently disabling the merge gate."
  }
]
```

**My take:** This is the one thing worth confirming before relying on the gate. The gitleaks-action handled checkout/ownership internally; a raw `docker run` doesn't. Two ways to harden it, both cheap:

- Add `-e GIT_CONFIG_COUNT=1 -e GIT_CONFIG_KEY_0=safe.directory -e GIT_CONFIG_VALUE_0=/repo` to the `docker run`, **or**
- Run betterleaks via its pre-commit hook in CI instead (reuses the config we already pinned), sidestepping the container entirely.

Everything else (pre-commit hook coordinates, doc references) is correct.

How would you like to proceed?

questions.question: The code-review flagged a possible 'dubious ownership' failure in the dockerized CI scan. How do you want to handle it before merge?

options.label: Add safe.directory env
options.description: Keep the container approach; pass safe.directory via GIT_CONFIG_* env vars to the docker run so git won't abort on the uid mismatch.
options.preview:
docker run --rm \
  -e GIT_CONFIG_COUNT=1 \
  -e GIT_CONFIG_KEY_0=safe.directory \
  -e GIT_CONFIG_VALUE_0=/repo \
  -v "$PWD:/repo" -w /repo \
  ghcr.io/betterleaks/betterleaks:v1.4.1 \
  git /repo --redact --verbose

options.label: Use pre-commit in CI
options.description: Drop the container; run betterleaks via `pre-commit run betterleaks` in CI, reusing the pinned hook config. No uid/root issue.
options.preview:
- uses: actions/setup-python@v5
- run: pip install pre-commit
- run: pre-commit run betterleaks \
    --all-files

options.label: Merge as-is
options.description: Accept the risk; merge now and fix CI later if the scan actually fails on the first run.

content.text:
Heads-up before I wire this in — one real supply-chain concern worth your eyes:

`dortort/betterleaks-action` is a **personal repo, 2 stars, created and last pushed the same day (2026-04-03), unmaintained since, at v0.1.0.** A third-party Action runs in your CI with access to `GITHUB_TOKEN` and repo contents, and here it'd be your merge-blocking *security* gate — so an unmaintained personal action is a slightly awkward trust anchor. Two mitigations I'm applying:

1. **Pin to the commit SHA**, not the `v0.1.0` tag (tags are mutable; a SHA can't be swapped under you).
2. **Pin the betterleaks `version` input to `v1.4.1`** instead of its `latest` default (matches our pre-commit pin; CLAUDE.md rule: don't track tools at "latest").

I'll keep `checkout@v6` (rest of the file uses it; your snippet had v4). Applying it:

toolUseResult.originalFile:
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

concurrency:
  group: ci-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  # ── Backend ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  backend-lint:
    name: Backend — lint & format
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install lint tools
        run: pip install -r backend/requirements-tooling.txt

      - name: Black (format check)
        run: black --check backend/

      - name: Ruff (lint)
        run: ruff check backend/

      - name: Typecheck-deps in sync
        # Guard the pre-commit mypy hook against backend/requirements-typecheck.txt
        # (Dependabot bumps the requirements file but bypasses the local hook —
        # this catches the drift in CI, which previously slipped through).
        run: python scripts/check-typecheck-deps.py

  backend-types:
    name: Backend — type check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install type-check deps
        # mypy from the tooling file; the typed subset it checks against.
        run: |
          pip install -r backend/requirements-tooling.txt
          pip install -r backend/requirements-typecheck.txt

      - name: mypy
        run: mypy backend/app/

  backend-sast:
    name: Backend — SAST (Bandit)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install Bandit
        run: pip install -r backend/requirements-tooling.txt

      - name: Bandit
        run: bandit -c pyproject.toml -r backend/app/

  backend-audit:
    name: Backend — dependency audit (pip-audit)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install pip-audit
        run: pip install pip-audit==2.10.0

      - name: pip-audit (full runtime + test surface)
        # Synchronous CVE gate over the entire runtime + test dependency tree
        # (single source: requirements-dev.txt) + transitive deps, against the
        # PyPI/OSV advisory DB. Complements the Dependabot alerts (full graph,
        # async) with a fail-fast merge check.
        #
        # The 5 ignored advisories are cryptography + pyOpenSSL CVEs blocked by
        # snowflake-connector-python<4 / snowflake-sqlalchemy<4 — a deliberate
        # major datasource bump tracked in #129 (the deadline for removing these).
        run: |
          pip-audit -r backend/requirements-dev.txt \
            --ignore-vuln PYSEC-2026-36 \
            --ignore-vuln PYSEC-2026-35 \
            --ignore-vuln CVE-2026-26007 \
            --ignore-vuln CVE-2026-27448 \
            --ignore-vuln CVE-2026-27459

  backend-test:
    name: Backend — tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Ephemeral Postgres for DB-backed integration tests (seed/run/endpoint).
    # Matches the compose image; gen_random_uuid() is built in to PG16.
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_USER: dataq
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: dataq
          POSTGRES_DB: dataq_test
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U dataq"
          --health-interval 5s
          --health-timeout 3s
          --health-retries 10
      # Redis broker for the real-broker E2E (dispatch -> worker round-trip).
      redis:
        image: redis:7-alpine
        ports:
          - 6379:6379
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "redis-cli ping"
          --health-interval 5s
          --health-timeout 3s
          --health-retries 10
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install test deps
        # Single source of truth: runtime + typed subset + test toolchain, all
        # from backend/requirements-dev.txt. pip upgraded for PYSEC-2026-196.
        run: |
          python -m pip install -U pip
          pip install -r backend/requirements-dev.txt

      - name: pytest
        # TEST_DATABASE_URL enables the db_session fixture; DATABASE_URL + REDIS_URL
        # enable the real-broker E2E. All DB/broker tests skip when unset (e.g. a
        # contributor running pytest without the services).
        env:
          TEST_DATABASE_URL: REDACTED
          DATABASE_URL: REDACTED
          REDIS_URL: redis://localhost:6379/0
        run: pytest backend/tests/

  # ── Secret scanning ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  secrets:
    name: Secret scanning (betterleaks)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      # Betterleaks has no official GitHub Action; run the pinned container image.
      # Drop-in gitleaks replacement: nonzero exit on findings fails the job.
      - name: Run betterleaks
        run: |
          docker run --rm -v "$PWD:/repo" -w /repo \
            ghcr.io/betterleaks/betterleaks:v1.4.1 \
            git /repo --redact --verbose

  # ── Frontend ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  frontend-lint:
    name: Frontend — lint & format
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v6
        with:
          version: 9

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: Prettier (format check)
        run: pnpm format:check

      - name: ESLint
        run: pnpm lint

      - name: TypeScript
        run: pnpm typecheck

  frontend-test:
    name: Frontend — tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v6
        with:
          version: 9

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: Vitest
        run: pnpm test

  frontend-audit:
    name: Frontend — dependency audit (pnpm audit)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v6
        with:
          version: 9

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: pnpm audit (high+)
        # Fails on high/critical advisories in the dependency tree; lower
        # severities are reported but don't block (avoid noise from unfixable
        # transitive dev-tool advisories). Pairs with Dependabot alerts (async).
        run: pnpm audit --audit-level=high

  # ── CodeQL SAST ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  codeql:
    name: CodeQL analysis
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      security-events: write
      actions: read
      contents: read
    strategy:
      matrix:
        language: [python, javascript-typescript]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: github/codeql-action/init@v4
        with:
          languages: ${{ matrix.language }}
          config-file: ./.github/codeql/codeql-config.yml

      - uses: github/codeql-action/autobuild@v4

      - uses: github/codeql-action/analyze@v4

  secrets:
    name: Secret scanning (betterleaks)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      # Betterleaks has no official GitHub Action; use the community action,
      # pinned to a commit SHA (not the mutable v0.1.0 tag) since it's a
      # third-party repo running with GITHUB_TOKEN. version input pinned too.

# Betterleaks has no official GitHub Action; run the official pinned
      # container image. Drop-in gitleaks replacement: nonzero exit on findings
      # fails the job. safe.directory env is required because the container runs
      # as root while checkout writes files as the runner user — without it git
      # aborts with "detected dubious ownership".
      - name: Run betterleaks
        run: |
          docker run --rm \
            -e GIT_CONFIG_COUNT=1 \
            -e GIT_CONFIG_KEY_0=safe.directory \
            -e GIT_CONFIG_VALUE_0=/repo \
            -v "$PWD:/repo" -w /repo \
            ghcr.io/betterleaks/betterleaks:v1.4.1 \
            git /repo --redact --verbose

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

concurrency:
  group: ci-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  # ── Backend ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  backend-lint:
    name: Backend — lint & format
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install lint tools
        run: pip install -r backend/requirements-tooling.txt

      - name: Black (format check)
        run: black --check backend/

      - name: Ruff (lint)
        run: ruff check backend/

      - name: Typecheck-deps in sync
        # Guard the pre-commit mypy hook against backend/requirements-typecheck.txt
        # (Dependabot bumps the requirements file but bypasses the local hook —
        # this catches the drift in CI, which previously slipped through).
        run: python scripts/check-typecheck-deps.py

  backend-types:
    name: Backend — type check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install type-check deps
        # mypy from the tooling file; the typed subset it checks against.
        run: |
          pip install -r backend/requirements-tooling.txt
          pip install -r backend/requirements-typecheck.txt

      - name: mypy
        run: mypy backend/app/

  backend-sast:
    name: Backend — SAST (Bandit)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install Bandit
        run: pip install -r backend/requirements-tooling.txt

      - name: Bandit
        run: bandit -c pyproject.toml -r backend/app/

  backend-audit:
    name: Backend — dependency audit (pip-audit)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install pip-audit
        run: pip install pip-audit==2.10.0

      - name: pip-audit (full runtime + test surface)
        # Synchronous CVE gate over the entire runtime + test dependency tree
        # (single source: requirements-dev.txt) + transitive deps, against the
        # PyPI/OSV advisory DB. Complements the Dependabot alerts (full graph,
        # async) with a fail-fast merge check.
        #
        # The 5 ignored advisories are cryptography + pyOpenSSL CVEs blocked by
        # snowflake-connector-python<4 / snowflake-sqlalchemy<4 — a deliberate
        # major datasource bump tracked in #129 (the deadline for removing these).
        run: |
          pip-audit -r backend/requirements-dev.txt \
            --ignore-vuln PYSEC-2026-36 \
            --ignore-vuln PYSEC-2026-35 \
            --ignore-vuln CVE-2026-26007 \
            --ignore-vuln CVE-2026-27448 \
            --ignore-vuln CVE-2026-27459

  backend-test:
    name: Backend — tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Ephemeral Postgres for DB-backed integration tests (seed/run/endpoint).
    # Matches the compose image; gen_random_uuid() is built in to PG16.
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_USER: dataq
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: dataq
          POSTGRES_DB: dataq_test
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U dataq"
          --health-interval 5s
          --health-timeout 3s
          --health-retries 10
      # Redis broker for the real-broker E2E (dispatch -> worker round-trip).
      redis:
        image: redis:7-alpine
        ports:
          - 6379:6379
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "redis-cli ping"
          --health-interval 5s
          --health-timeout 3s
          --health-retries 10
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install test deps
        # Single source of truth: runtime + typed subset + test toolchain, all
        # from backend/requirements-dev.txt. pip upgraded for PYSEC-2026-196.
        run: |
          python -m pip install -U pip
          pip install -r backend/requirements-dev.txt

      - name: pytest
        # TEST_DATABASE_URL enables the db_session fixture; DATABASE_URL + REDIS_URL
        # enable the real-broker E2E. All DB/broker tests skip when unset (e.g. a
        # contributor running pytest without the services).
        env:
          TEST_DATABASE_URL: REDACTED
          DATABASE_URL: REDACTED
          REDIS_URL: redis://localhost:6379/0
        run: pytest backend/tests/

  # ── Secret scanning ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  secrets:
    name: Secret scanning (betterleaks)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      # Betterleaks has no official GitHub Action; use the community action,
      # pinned to a commit SHA (not the mutable v0.1.0 tag) since it's a
      # third-party repo running with GITHUB_TOKEN. version input pinned too.
      - name: Run betterleaks
        uses: dortort/betterleaks-action@cd0b905aa4befb8f42f8b1ab98150180c9e77515 # v0.1.0
        with:
          version: v1.4.1
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

  # ── Frontend ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  frontend-lint:
    name: Frontend — lint & format
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v6
        with:
          version: 9

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: Prettier (format check)
        run: pnpm format:check

      - name: ESLint
        run: pnpm lint

      - name: TypeScript
        run: pnpm typecheck

  frontend-test:
    name: Frontend — tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v6
        with:
          version: 9

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: Vitest
        run: pnpm test

  frontend-audit:
    name: Frontend — dependency audit (pnpm audit)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v6
        with:
          version: 9

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: pnpm audit (high+)
        # Fails on high/critical advisories in the dependency tree; lower
        # severities are reported but don't block (avoid noise from unfixable
        # transitive dev-tool advisories). Pairs with Dependabot alerts (async).
        run: pnpm audit --audit-level=high

  # ── CodeQL SAST ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  codeql:
    name: CodeQL analysis
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      security-events: write
      actions: read
      contents: read
    strategy:
      matrix:
        language: [python, javascript-typescript]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - uses: github/codeql-action/init@v4
        with:
          languages: ${{ matrix.language }}
          config-file: ./.github/codeql/codeql-config.yml

      - uses: github/codeql-action/autobuild@v4

      - uses: github/codeql-action/analyze@v4

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# One-command dev environment setup from a fresh clone.
# Usage: ./scripts/setup.sh
# Requires: conda (miniconda/miniforge), docker, pnpm, git

set -euo pipefail

CYAN='\033[0;36m'; GREEN='\033[0;32m'; RED='\033[0;31m'; NC='\033[0m'
step() { echo -e "${CYAN}▶ $1${NC}"; }
ok()   { echo -e "${GREEN}✓ $1${NC}"; }
die()  { echo -e "${RED}✗ $1${NC}" >&2; exit 1; }

# ── Prerequisites ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Checking prerequisites"
command -v conda  >/dev/null || die "conda not found — install miniconda or miniforge first"
command -v docker >/dev/null || die "docker not found"
command -v pnpm   >/dev/null || die "pnpm not found — run: npm install -g pnpm"
command -v git    >/dev/null || die "git not found"
ok "Prerequisites OK"

# ── Conda environment ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Creating / updating conda environment 'dataq'"
if conda env list | grep -q "^dataq "; then
  conda env update -n dataq -f environment.yml --prune
  ok "Conda env updated"
else
  conda env create -f environment.yml
  ok "Conda env created"
fi

Based on the search results, here's what I found about pre-commit's golang language support:

## Go Installation Behavior

As of version 3.0.0, pre-commit will bootstrap Go if it is not present. This represents an important change—previously you were required to have Go already installed on your system.

However, the behavior varies depending on your pre-commit version:

- **Pre-commit 3.0.0+**: Go is automatically installed if needed
- **Pre-commit earlier than 3.0.0**: You must have Go already installed on your system

## Language Support

Golang hooks are known to work on any system which has Go installed, and have been tested on Linux, macOS, and Windows.

## Hook Execution

The hook repository must contain Go source code, which will be installed via `go install ./...`. Pre-commit will create an isolated GOPATH for each hook and the entry should match an executable which will get installed into the GOPATH's bin directory.

So in summary: if you're using pre-commit 3.0.0 or later, Go will be installed automatically if needed. For earlier versions, you'll need Go pre-installed on your system.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# One-command dev environment setup from a fresh clone.
# Usage: ./scripts/setup.sh
# Requires: conda (miniconda/miniforge), docker, pnpm, git

set -euo pipefail

CYAN='\033[0;36m'; GREEN='\033[0;32m'; RED='\033[0;31m'; NC='\033[0m'
step() { echo -e "${CYAN}▶ $1${NC}"; }
ok()   { echo -e "${GREEN}✓ $1${NC}"; }
die()  { echo -e "${RED}✗ $1${NC}" >&2; exit 1; }

# ── Prerequisites ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Checking prerequisites"
command -v conda  >/dev/null || die "conda not found — install miniconda or miniforge first"
command -v docker >/dev/null || die "docker not found"
command -v pnpm   >/dev/null || die "pnpm not found — run: npm install -g pnpm"
command -v git    >/dev/null || die "git not found"
ok "Prerequisites OK"

# ── Conda environment ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Creating / updating conda environment 'dataq'"
if conda env list | grep -q "^dataq "; then
  conda env update -n dataq -f environment.yml --prune
  ok "Conda env updated"
else
  conda env create -f environment.yml
  ok "Conda env created"
fi

# ── Pre-commit hooks ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Installing pre-commit hooks"
conda run -n dataq pre-commit install --install-hooks
ok "Pre-commit hooks installed"

# ── Frontend dependencies ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Installing frontend dependencies (pnpm)"
(cd frontend && pnpm install)
ok "Frontend dependencies installed"

# ── Docker services ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Starting Docker services (Postgres, Redis)"
docker compose up -d postgres redis
ok "Docker services started"

# ── Database migrations ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Waiting for Postgres to be ready"
for i in $(seq 1 30); do
  if docker compose exec -T postgres pg_isready -U dataq >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    ok "Postgres ready"
    break
  fi
  [ "$i" -eq 30 ] && die "Postgres did not become ready in time"
  sleep 1
done

step "Running Alembic migrations"
conda run -n dataq sh -c "cd backend && alembic upgrade head"
ok "Migrations applied"

# ── Seed data ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Seeding dev data"
if [ -f backend/scripts/seed_dev.py ]; then
  conda run -n dataq python backend/scripts/seed_dev.py
  ok "Dev data seeded"
else
  echo "  (no seed script yet — skipping)"
fi

# ── Done ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
echo ""
echo -e "${GREEN}━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━${NC}"
echo -e "${GREEN}  DataQ dev environment ready!${NC}"
echo -e "${GREEN}━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━${NC}"
echo ""
echo "  Next steps:"
echo "    conda activate dataq"
echo "    docker compose up          # start all services"
echo "    # API: http://localhost:8000/docs"
echo "    # UI:  http://localhost:3000"
echo ""

+# can take a minute. pre-commit (≥3.0) bootstraps its own Go — no system Go needed.

After the gitleaks → betterleaks switch, existing clones must re-run
`pre-commit install --install-hooks` to pick up the new hook. Note that the
first run compiles the Go hook and that pre-commit (≥3.0, repo pins 4.2.0)
bootstraps Go itself, so no system Go is required.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push -u origin docs/betterleaks-hook-refresh-note 2>&1 | tail -2

## What

Follow-up to #153 (gitleaks → betterleaks). Documents the one operational detail of the switch: existing clones need to refresh their installed hooks.

## Changes

- **`CONTRIBUTING.md`** — local-dev block now shows `pre-commit install --install-hooks` as the post-hook-change refresh, noting the first run compiles the betterleaks Go hook and that pre-commit (≥3.0) bootstraps Go itself.
- **`scripts/setup.sh`** — comment on the pre-commit step so a fresh clone isn't surprised by the one-time Go compile.

No system Go prerequisite is added — repo pins `pre-commit==4.2.0`, which auto-installs Go for `language: golang` hooks. Fresh clones already get this via `setup.sh`.

Merged. Both PRs are now on `main`:

- **[#153](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/153)** — gitleaks → betterleaks (pre-commit + CI, official container)
- **[#154](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/154)** — the setup/refresh docs

**What the docs now say:**
- [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md#L176) — existing clones run `pre-commit install --install-hooks` to pick up the new hook; first run compiles the betterleaks Go hook; **no system Go needed** (pre-commit 4.2.0 bootstraps it).
- [scripts/setup.sh:31](scripts/setup.sh#L31) — comment flagging the one-time Go compile so fresh clones aren't surprised by the slower step.

The key correction from my earlier offer: I'd assumed contributors might need Go installed, but since the repo pins `pre-commit==4.2.0` (≥3.0), pre-commit handles the Go toolchain itself — so the only action for existing contributors is the hook refresh, which the docs now cover.

Sources: [pre-commit.com](https://pre-commit.com/)

file.content:
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: ${POSTGRES_USER:-dataq}
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:-dataq}
      POSTGRES_DB: ${POSTGRES_DB:-dataq}
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U ${POSTGRES_USER:-dataq}"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10

  # FastAPI + Celery share one image (backend/Dockerfile, conda env `dataq`).
  # DATABASE_URL / REDIS_URL are overridden here to the in-network service
  # hostnames (the .env defaults point at localhost for host-side dev).
  api:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
    command: uvicorn backend.app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    env_file:
      - path: .env
        required: false
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: REDACTED
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379/0
      # SECRET_STORE defaults to `env` (per-process). For connection-driven worker
      # runs (Week 5) that resolve a credential the API wrote at runtime, set
      # SECRET_STORE=redis on BOTH api and worker so the write is shared across
      # processes (#86). Dev only — plaintext in Redis. Leaves the probe's
      # KV_SECRET_* env path unaffected when left at the default.
    volumes:
      # Bind-mount source for hot reload; the conda env in the image is untouched.
      - ./backend:/workspace/backend
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy

  worker:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
    command: celery -A backend.app.worker.celery_app worker --loglevel=INFO
    env_file:
      - path: .env
        required: false
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: REDACTED
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379/0
      # See the api service: set SECRET_STORE=redis here too for cross-process
      # secret sharing with the API (#86). Default `env` is per-process.
    volumes:
      - ./backend:/workspace/backend
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy

volumes:
  postgres_data:

file.content:
(cd frontend && pnpm install)
ok "Frontend dependencies installed"

# ── Docker services ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Starting Docker services (Postgres, Redis)"
docker compose up -d postgres redis
ok "Docker services started"

# ── Database migrations ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Waiting for Postgres to be ready"
for i in $(seq 1 30); do
  if docker compose exec -T postgres pg_isready -U dataq >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    ok "Postgres ready"
    break
  fi
  [ "$i" -eq 30 ] && die "Postgres did not become ready in time"
  sleep 1
done

step "Running Alembic migrations"
conda run -n dataq sh -c "cd backend && alembic upgrade head"
ok "Migrations applied"

# ── Seed data ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step "Seeding dev data"
if [ -f backend/scripts/seed_dev.py ]; then
  conda run -n dataq python backend/scripts/seed_dev.py
  ok "Dev data seeded"
else
  echo "  (no seed script yet — skipping)"
fi

# ── Done ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
echo ""
echo -e "${GREEN}━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━${NC}"
echo -e "${GREEN}  DataQ dev environment ready!${NC}"
echo -e "${GREEN}━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━${NC}"
echo ""
echo "  Next steps:"
echo "    conda activate dataq"
echo "    docker compose up          # start all services"
echo "    # API: http://localhost:8000/docs"
echo "    # UI:  http://localhost:3000"
echo ""

content.content:
1	# DataQ backend image — serves both the FastAPI API and the Celery worker.
2	# Build context is the repo ROOT (not backend/) because the app imports as the
3	# top-level `backend.app.*` package and environment.yml lives at the root:
4	#   docker build -f backend/Dockerfile .
5	#
6	# Conda-only per CLAUDE.md (no venv/poetry). The dataq env is created from the
7	# pinned environment.yml and its bin dir is put first on PATH so `uvicorn`,
8	# `celery`, `alembic`, and `python` resolve to it without a `conda run` wrapper
9	# (which would interfere with signal forwarding to uvicorn/celery).

15	# environment.yml changes, not on every code edit.

file.content:
# DataQ backend image — serves both the FastAPI API and the Celery worker.
# Build context is the repo ROOT (not backend/) because the app imports as the
# top-level `backend.app.*` package and environment.yml lives at the root:
#   docker build -f backend/Dockerfile .
#
# Conda-only per CLAUDE.md (no venv/poetry). The dataq env is created from the
# pinned environment.yml and its bin dir is put first on PATH so `uvicorn`,
# `celery`, `alembic`, and `python` resolve to it without a `conda run` wrapper
# (which would interfere with signal forwarding to uvicorn/celery).
FROM continuumio/miniconda3:24.11.1-0

WORKDIR /workspace

# Create the conda env first and cache the layer — only re-runs when the pinned
# environment.yml changes, not on every code edit.
COPY environment.yml ./
RUN conda env create -f environment.yml && conda clean -afy

ENV PATH=/opt/conda/envs/dataq/bin:$PATH \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# Application code + tooling config (pyproject is needed to run pytest/mypy in-container).
COPY pyproject.toml ./
COPY backend ./backend

EXPOSE 8000

# Default to the API; the worker service overrides `command` in compose.
CMD ["uvicorn", "backend.app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

input.description: Check vite port, seed script, related issues

lastPrompt: What is covered in docker compose so far, what is pending, and what is the plan to add those missing components?

content.text:
Here's the full picture, cross-checked against the compose file, the Dockerfile, setup.sh, and the docs.

## ✅ Covered today — 4 services in [docker-compose.yml](docker-compose.yml)

| Service | Image / build | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `postgres` | `postgres:16-alpine` | healthcheck + persistent `postgres_data` volume |
| `redis` | `redis:7-alpine` | healthcheck |
| `api` | `backend/Dockerfile` (conda `dataq`) | uvicorn `--reload`, source bind-mounted for hot reload, waits on pg/redis healthy |
| `worker` | same image | Celery worker, same deps/healthcheck gating |

The API and worker correctly share one image and override `command`. Service-to-service `DATABASE_URL`/`REDIS_URL` are wired to the in-network hostnames. This part is solid.

## ⚠️ Pending / missing

**1. Frontend (React/Vite) service — the real gap.** Every doc claims `docker compose up` starts a React dev server — [CLAUDE.md:196](CLAUDE.md#L196) ("…+ React + Celery worker"), [CONTRIBUTING.md:168](CONTRIBUTING.md#L168) ("React dev server"), and [setup.sh:82](scripts/setup.sh#L82) even prints `UI: http://localhost:3000`. But **there is no `frontend` service and no frontend Dockerfile.** Today the UI only runs via `cd frontend && pnpm dev` on the host (Vite is configured for port 3000 in [vite.config.ts:16](frontend/vite.config.ts#L16)). This is a docs-vs-reality drift.

**2. Migrations don't run on `compose up`.** `alembic upgrade head` only runs once inside [setup.sh:60](scripts/setup.sh#L60). There's no migration init-container or API entrypoint, so after you add a migration (or wipe the volume) and just `docker compose up`, the API boots against an un-migrated DB.

**3. Seed step is a no-op.** setup.sh guards on `backend/scripts/seed_dev.py`, which **doesn't exist** — so "seed dev data" silently skips.

**4. `SECRET_STORE=redis` not actually set.** It's only a comment in both services; default stays per-process `env`, so the cross-process secret sharing that Week-5 worker runs need (#86) isn't wired by default.

**5. Minor:** no `restart:` policies; no healthchecks on `api`/`worker` themselves (only pg/redis).

## Suggested plan

| # | Change | Effort |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Add a `frontend` service: `node:20-alpine`, mount `./frontend`, `pnpm install && pnpm dev --host`, expose `3000:3000`, `depends_on: api`. (Dev-server container — a prod multi-stage build is a separate, later concern.) | S |
| 2 | Add a `migrate` init service (same backend image, `command: alembic upgrade head`, `depends_on: postgres healthy`) and make `api`/`worker` depend on it `condition: service_completed_successfully`. | S |
| 3 | Either create `backend/scripts/seed_dev.py` or drop the seed line + the `UI:3000` promise until the frontend service lands, so docs match reality. | S |
| 4 | Decide on #4: set `SECRET_STORE=redis` by default in compose, or leave as opt-in and document it. | XS |

Items 1–3 each map to a separate one-functionality PR per the working agreements.

One thing I'd flag before doing any of it: **#1 and #3 are doc drift that contradicts committed docs** — per the working agreement (defects → GitHub issue, no silent fixes), those should get an issue first. Note [#128](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/128) (full-stack E2E) is adjacent but not the same thing.

Want me to (a) file the issue(s) for the frontend-service / seed drift, and/or (b) start with the migrate init-container or the frontend service as the first PR?

input.command:
gh issue create --title "docker-compose: frontend (React/Vite) service is documented but missing" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem

Multiple docs claim \`docker compose up\` starts a React dev server, but **no \`frontend\` service exists** in [docker-compose.yml](../blob/main/docker-compose.yml) and there is no frontend Dockerfile.

Doc-vs-reality drift:
- [CLAUDE.md L196](../blob/main/CLAUDE.md#L196) — \`# Postgres + Redis + FastAPI + React + Celery worker\`
- [CONTRIBUTING.md L168](../blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#L168) — \`# starts Postgres, Redis, FastAPI, React dev server, Celery worker\`
- [scripts/setup.sh L82](../blob/main/scripts/setup.sh#L82) — prints \`UI: http://localhost:3000\`

Today the UI only runs on the host via \`cd frontend && pnpm dev\` (Vite configured for port 3000 in \`frontend/vite.config.ts\`).

## Proposed fix

Add a \`frontend\` dev service (e.g. \`node:20-alpine\`, mount \`./frontend\`, \`pnpm install && pnpm dev --host\`, publish \`3000:3000\`, \`depends_on: api\`). A production multi-stage build is a separate, later concern.

Until then, the docs overstate what compose does.

## Scope

Standalone PR (one functionality). Related but distinct from #128 (full-stack E2E).
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -1

gh issue create --title "docker-compose: DB migrations not applied on \`compose up\` (only in setup.sh)" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem

\`alembic upgrade head\` runs only once inside [scripts/setup.sh L60](../blob/main/scripts/setup.sh#L60). There is no migration init-container or API entrypoint step.

Consequence: after adding a new migration (or wiping the \`postgres_data\` volume), a plain \`docker compose up\` boots the \`api\` and \`worker\` against an **un-migrated database**. Only re-running \`setup.sh\` fixes it, which is non-obvious.

## Proposed fix

Add a one-shot \`migrate\` service (same backend image, \`command: alembic upgrade head\`, \`depends_on: postgres\` healthy) and have \`api\`/\`worker\` \`depends_on: migrate\` with \`condition: service_completed_successfully\`.

## Scope

Standalone PR (one functionality).
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -1

gh issue create --title "setup.sh seed step is a silent no-op (backend/scripts/seed_dev.py missing)" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem

[scripts/setup.sh L65](../blob/main/scripts/setup.sh#L65) guards the seed step on \`backend/scripts/seed_dev.py\`, which **does not exist**, so setup prints \`(no seed script yet — skipping)\` and a fresh dev DB has no baseline data.

Both [CONTRIBUTING.md L105](../blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#L105) and the README describe setup as ending with "seed data", so this is doc-vs-reality drift.

## Proposed fix

Add \`backend/scripts/seed_dev.py\` that idempotently seeds a minimal dev baseline (e.g. a dev user + a sample suite/connection) so the seed step actually does something. Fixed alongside the compose hardening work.
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -1

file.content:
from functools import lru_cache
from typing import Literal

from pydantic import Field
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict


class Settings(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
        env_file=".env",
        env_file_encoding="utf-8",
        extra="ignore",
        case_sensitive=False,
    )

    environment: Literal["dev", "staging", "prod"] = "dev"
    log_level: Literal["DEBUG", "INFO", "WARNING", "ERROR", "CRITICAL"] = "INFO"

    database_url: str = Field(default="REDACTED")
    redis_url: str = Field(default="redis://localhost:6379/0")

    applicationinsights_connection_string: str | None = None

    sample_failures_retention_days: int = 30

    azure_tenant_id: str | None = None
    azure_api_client_id: str | None = None
    azure_spa_client_id: str | None = None
    azure_api_scope: str = "user_impersonation"

    auth_dev_bypass: bool = False

    secret_store: Literal["env", "redis", "azure_key_vault"] = (
        "env"  # noqa: S105 — mode selector, not a password
    )
    azure_key_vault_url: str | None = None

    # SecretStore key holding the ADF webhook shared secret (ADR 0006). Resolved
    # via SecretStore.get → EnvSecretStore maps it to KV_SECRET_ADF_WEBHOOK_SECRET
    # in dev, Key Vault secret `adf-webhook-secret` in prod. Not the secret value.
    adf_webhook_secret_name: str = "adf-webhook-secret"  # noqa: S105 — KV key name, not a secret
    # SecretStore key holding the Airflow callback HMAC signing key (ADR 0007).
    # → KV_SECRET_AIRFLOW_WEBHOOK_SECRET in dev, KV secret `airflow-webhook-secret`
    # in prod. The signing key, not a webhook value.
    airflow_webhook_secret_name: str = "airflow-webhook-secret"  # noqa: S105 — KV key name

    # ── Snowflake probe (Week 1 exit-gate endpoint) ──────────────────────────
    # Config for the single seeded dev Snowflake connection the probe runs
    # against. All optional: when unset the probe still creates + dispatches a
    # run, which then fails-soft (no live warehouse). secret_ref names the
    # SecretStore entry holding the password (e.g. KV_SECRET_SNOWFLAKE_DEV).
    probe_snowflake_account: str | None = None
    probe_snowflake_user: str | None = None
    probe_snowflake_database: str | None = None
    probe_snowflake_schema: str | None = None
    probe_snowflake_warehouse: str | None = None
    probe_snowflake_role: str | None = None
    probe_snowflake_table: str | None = None
    probe_snowflake_secret_ref: str | None = None

    @property
    def azure_auth_configured(self) -> bool:
        return bool(self.azure_tenant_id and self.azure_api_client_id)

    @property
    def azure_api_scope_uri(self) -> str | None:
        if not self.azure_api_client_id:
            return None
        return f"api://{self.azure_api_client_id}/{self.azure_api_scope}"


@lru_cache(maxsize=1)
def get_settings() -> Settings:
    return Settings()
